verses.commentary: Teaching in the temple, preaching the gospel. Chief priests, scribes, elders confront him. Formal delegation.
'Tell us by what authority you do these things — who is it that gave you this authority?' Credential-check after the temple-cleansing.
'I will also ask you a question — tell me:'
'The baptism of John — was it from heaven or from man?' Counter-question.
They discussed: 'If we say from heaven, he will say, Why did you not believe him?'
'If we say from man, all the people will stone us — they are convinced John was a prophet.' Political calculation, not theological inquiry.
'We do not know where it was from.' Calculated cowardice.
'Neither will I tell you by what authority I do these things.' Refusal as judgment — they have disqualified themselves from disclosure.
Parable: a man planted a vineyard, let it out to tenants, went away for a long time. Echo of Isaiah 5 — God's vineyard.
At harvest sent a servant to receive fruit — tenants beat him, sent him away empty.
Another servant — beat, treated shamefully, empty-handed.
A third — wounded, cast out. Escalating violence.
Owner: 'What shall I do? I will send my beloved son (huion mou ton agapeton, υἱόν μου τὸν ἀγαπητόν) — perhaps they will respect him.' Christological self-reference. 'Beloved' echoes baptismal voice.
Tenants saw the son — discussed: 'This is the heir. Let us kill him so the inheritance may be ours.' Cynical strategy stated in plain view.
Threw him out of the vineyard and killed him. (Echo of Hebrews 13:12 — Jesus killed 'outside the gate.') What will the owner do?
Come and destroy those tenants, give the vineyard to others. People hearing: 'May it never be!' (me genoito, μὴ γένοιτο). Religious protest at the parable's verdict.
Jesus looked at them: 'What is this written: The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone?' (Psalm 118:22). Rejected stone vindicated.
Everyone who falls on that stone will be broken; on whom it falls, it will crush him. Stone-Christology with two judgments.
Scribes and chief priests sought to lay hands on him that very hour — but feared the people, for they perceived he had told the parable against them. Self-recognition without repentance.
Watching him, they sent spies pretending to be sincere — to catch him in something he said, to deliver him to the authority and jurisdiction of the governor. Strategic entrapment.
'Teacher, we know you speak and teach rightly — show no partiality — truly teach the way of God.' Flattery as setup.
'Is it lawful for us to give tribute (phoron, φόρον) to Caesar, or not?' Same trap as Matthew 22:17.
Perceiving their craftiness (panourgia, πανουργίαν), he said:
'Show me a denarius. Whose likeness and inscription does it have?' 'Caesar's.'
'Then render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's.' Dual-sovereignty resolution.
They could not catch him in what he said before the people — marveled at his answer, became silent. Round one to Jesus.
Some Sadducees — who deny resurrection — came to him with a question. New round, different opponents.
'Teacher, Moses wrote: if a man's brother dies, having a wife but no children, the man must take the widow and raise up offspring for his brother.' Levirate marriage (Deuteronomy 25:5).
Seven brothers — the first took a wife, died childless.
Second married her.
Third — same — through all seven, leaving no children.
Last, the woman also died.
'In the resurrection, whose wife will she be — all seven had her as wife?' Trap by absurdity.
'The sons of this age marry and are given in marriage.'
But those considered worthy of that age and the resurrection from the dead neither marry nor are given in marriage.
Cannot die anymore — they are equal to angels and are sons of God, being sons of the resurrection. New mode of existence.
Moses showed (at the bush) that the dead are raised, when he called the Lord 'God of Abraham, Isaac, Jacob' (Exodus 3:6). Present tense — they live.
He is not God of the dead but of the living — for to him all live. Tight exegesis from a text the Sadducees accept.
Some of the scribes: 'Teacher, you have spoken well.' Even rival Pharisaic-leaning scribes approve when the Sadducees are bested.
No one dared to ask him any more questions. End of cross-examination.
Now Jesus' counter-question: 'How is it said the Christ is David's son?'
David himself says in the Psalms: 'The Lord said to my Lord: Sit at my right hand —' (Psalm 110:1).
'Until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet.'
David calls him 'Lord' — how is he his son? Messiah is more than biological descendant. The puzzle remains; Jesus does not resolve it here, but his identity does.
In the hearing of all the people — he said to his disciples:
Beware of the scribes who like to walk in long robes and love greetings in the marketplaces, the best seats in the synagogues, places of honor at feasts.
Who devour widows' houses, for a pretense make long prayers — they will receive the greater condemnation. Religious appearance as cover for exploitation. Compressed version of Matthew 23.
